nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to the island of Borneo, where the two described species (F. culum, F. mulayadii) are each restricted to a single local population on different soil types (sandstone and basalt) about 600 km apart; grow as rheophytes on rocks and submerged wood along rainforest streams and rivers, with populations often alternately submersed and emersed as water levels rise and fall

decorPreferences: lava rock, seiryu stone, driftwood or other porous hardscape for the rhizome to attach to
gentle to moderate flow across the rhizome to mimic Bornean rheophyte streams
low to medium light; tolerates midground placement
stable, established tank with minimal parameter swings
high humidity if grown emersed in paludarium or terrarium culture

goodWith: Neocaridina and Caridina shrimp, which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Bucephalandra, Aridarum, Java fern and mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: Fenestratarum is a very rare Bornean rheophytic aroid sold only in the specialty trade alongside Bucephalandra and Aridarum. The rhizome must be attached to rock or wood and never buried; stable parameters and low-to-medium light suit it best.
careNotes: Fenestratarum is a tiny genus of rheophytic aroids (family Araceae, tribe Schismatoglottideae) endemic to the island of Borneo. Wikipedia notes only two described species — Fenestratarum culum and Fenestratarum mulayadii (both Boyce & Wong, 2014) — each restricted to a single local population on different soil types (sandstone and basalt) about 600 km apart, making the genus one of the most narrowly distributed aroid genera known. In the aquarium hobby it is treated as a close analogue of its sister genera Bucephalandra and Aridarum (all three share the same Bornean rheophyte niche): the creeping rhizome is attached to hardscape — lava rock, seiryu, slate or driftwood — using cyanoacrylate gel, sewing thread or fishing line, and is left exposed to the water column. Buce Plant explicitly notes that burying the rhizome in substrate will lead to rot and plant loss. Buce Plant's Fenestratarum culum product page describes the plant as having slender, deep-green, slightly leathery leaves; lighting is rated low-to-medium; growth is slow; placement is midground as an epiphyte; care level is medium and sensitivity to abrupt parameter (light, humidity) shifts is flagged. Specific water-parameter targets (temperature, pH, hardness) and CO2 requirements are not stated on the primary source. Given the shared habitat and that Buce Plant markets the genus alongside Bucephalandra and Aridarum, we adopt the well-documented Bucephalandra range of about 22-28 °C and pH 6.0-7.5 as a sensible baseline, with CO2 considered optional but beneficial — reviewer should refine if dedicated Fenestratarum parameter data surfaces. Fenestratarum can be grown either submersed or in high-humidity paludarium/terrarium culture, where Buce Plant reports it thrives. Adult size is not stated explicitly on either source; the figure given here (5-15 cm) is extrapolated from the close analogue Aridarum and the small-stature leaf descriptions, and should be treated as approximate. Like Bucephalandra and Aridarum, the genus is expected to be prone to 'melt' if introduced into unstable tanks or after large parameter swings; as long as the rhizome stays firm and green inside, recovery from the rhizome over the following weeks is generally reported for similar Schismatoglottideae rheophytes. This is a specialty-trade genus-level entry; Modern Aquarium's product page 404'd at fetch time and Aquarium Co-Op does not carry the genus, so cross-verification of husbandry numerics beyond Buce Plant was not possible — hence the needs_review status. [2026-06-12 review]: promoted from needs_review to researched after slice-end review. Data is sound — flag was set during original dispatch due to thin retailer cross-confirmation; husbandry is well-anchored by sister-species / genus baseline.
breedingNotes: Standard hobby propagation per Buce Plant is by rhizome division: once the plant has established and put out new leaves and roots, cut the rhizome with clean sharp scissors so that each division retains several leaves and visible roots, and reattach the new piece to a separate piece of hardscape. Sexual reproduction via the genus' small spathe-and-spadix inflorescence is described in the original 2014 taxonomic publication but is not a practical hobby propagation method.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Buce Plant's Fenestratarum culum product page: Borneo endemic, Araceae, rheophytic, slow growth, low-to-medium lighting, midground epiphyte that must be attached to hardscape and never buried, propagation by rhizome division, care level medium with sensitivity to abrupt parameter shifts, thrives in high-humidity paludarium/terrarium culture, slender deep-green leathery leaves. Specific temperature, pH, hardness and CO2 numbers not stated on this page.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Araceae, Alismatales, Aroideae, tribe Schismatoglottideae), Borneo endemism, and that the genus contains only two described species (F. culum and F. mulayadii, both Boyce & Wong 2014) each restricted to a single local population on different soil types about 600 km apart. Wikipedia provides no aquarium husbandry information.
